4. pairs_to_chat_messages missing Raises 📘 Rule violation ✧ Quality
>
>Description >
> >
>The public function pairs_to_chat_messages docstring includes Args: and Returns: but does not
>include a Raises: section. This violates the requirement that public callables have fully
>structured docstrings.
>
>
>
>Code >
> >[libs/model-training/src/model_training/d2l_data.py[R928-970]](https://github.com/ElixirTrials/rune/pull/28/files#diff-d4343ba63bc815bbf06209f8eec1948017820af07a2891da6fa7a86b6a6a6660R928-R970) > >```diff >+def pairs_to_chat_messages( >+ pairs: list[dict[str, Any]], >+ *, >+ mode: Literal["multi_turn", "single_turn"] = "multi_turn", >+ system_prompt: str = SYSTEM_PROMPT, >+) -> tuple[list[list[dict[str, str]]], list[dict[str, str]]]: >+ r"""Convert mined pair records into SFT chat conversations. >+ >+ ``multi_turn`` (preferred when pairs share a ``source_task_id``): emits >+ one conversation per task grouping — ``[system, user_1, assistant_1, >+ user_2, assistant_2, ...]`` — where each (user, assistant) pair is one >+ review→revision cycle. This preserves the attempt-error-correction >+ structure so the adapter can encode the trajectory rather than only the >+ final code. >+ >+ ``single_turn``: emits one conversation per pair (no clustering). Useful >+ when ``metadata.source_task_id`` is missing or the caller prefers flat >+ examples. Each conversation is ``[system, user, assistant]``. >+ >+ Pairs are grouped in the order they appear in the input; within a group, >+ pairs are sorted by ``metadata.step_index`` to preserve chronological >+ order of review cycles. Empty input returns ``([], [])``. >+ >+ Args: >+ pairs: List of pair records as emitted by ``normalize_mined_pairs``. >+ Each record must have ``activation_text`` and ``teacher_text``. >+ ``metadata.source_task_id`` and ``metadata.step_index`` are used >+ for grouping/ordering when present; fall back to ``task_id``. >+ mode: ``"multi_turn"`` clusters pairs by task_id; ``"single_turn"`` >+ emits one conversation per pair. >+ system_prompt: System message for every conversation. >+ >+ Returns: >+ A tuple ``(conversations, pre_post_records)`` where: >+ >+ - ``conversations`` is a list of chat-message lists suitable for >+ ``datasets.Dataset.from_list([{"messages": m} for m in convs])`` >+ and TRL's ``SFTTrainer`` with ``assistant_only_loss=True``. >+ - ``pre_post_records`` is a list of ``{"pre_code": str, "post_code": >+ str}`` dicts aligned 1:1 with ``conversations``. For multi-turn >+ conversations the pre/post codes are the concatenation (joined by >+ ``"\\n\\n"``) of each individual turn's pre/post code. >+ """ >``` >

def _prune_retained_adapters(
    study: Any, run_args: HPORunArgs
) -> list[tuple[int, float]]:
    """Delete adapter dirs for all but the top-K trials by fitness value."""
    import shutil  # noqa: PLC0415

    completed = [
        t
        for t in study.get_trials(deepcopy=False)
        if t.state.name == "COMPLETE" and t.value is not None
    ]
    ranked = sorted(completed, key=lambda t: t.value, reverse=True)
    keep = {t.number for t in ranked[: run_args.keep_top_k]}
    removed: list[tuple[int, float]] = []
    for t in ranked[run_args.keep_top_k :]:
        adapter_dir = run_args.output_root / f"trial_{t.number:03d}"
        if adapter_dir.exists():
            shutil.rmtree(adapter_dir, ignore_errors=True)
            removed.append((t.number, t.value))
    logger.info(
        "Retention: kept %d, pruned %d (keep_top_k=%d)",
        len(keep),

def mlflow_log_params(params: dict[str, Any]) -> None:
    """Log a dict of params to the active MLflow run. Silent no-op on failure.

    All exceptions are caught and logged at DEBUG level so that MLflow
    unavailability never interrupts training.

Here's the full audit of all 13 qodo items vs current HEAD (`1ebf62b`):

## ✅ Addressed (5 items)

| # | Item | Status |
|---|------|--------|
| 3 | `trainer_cli.main()` docstring | Has `Args/Returns/Raises` (commit `6a26b54`) |
| 5 | `run_training_hpo.main()` docstring | Has `Args/Returns/Raises` (commit `6a26b54`) |
| 6 | 88-char log line wrap | Wrapped across adjacent string literals (commit `6a26b54`) |
| 7 | `diff_loss.py` fallback uses identity weights | `[1.0 if lab != -100 else 0.0 ...]` (commit `e1d4310`) |
| 8 | HPO heldout evaluator 4-bit NF4 + `device_map="auto"` + `attention_mask` | All present at `run_training_hpo.py:464-472` (commit `e1d4310`) |

## ⚠️ Wontfix (1 item) — resolved via qodo UI, not code comment

| # | Item | Status |
|---|------|--------|
| 1 | `shutil.rmtree` on trial adapter dirs | PR summary marks wontfix ("trial artifacts are disposable"). No justifying comment in `_prune_retained_adapters` itself; resolution was via the qodo thread, not source. |

## 🔴 Regressed by my griffe fix last turn (2 items)

| # | Item | Current state |
|---|------|---------------|
| 2 | `mlflow_log_params` structured docstring | My griffe fix removed `Returns:` and `Raises:` sections, keeping only `Args:`. Qodo rule 127857 originally wanted all three; my commit `1ebf62b` undid that. |
| 4 | `pairs_to_chat_messages` `Raises:` section | My griffe fix removed the explicit `Raises:` block entirely. Qodo's note explicitly said "even when no exceptions, rule requires explicit `Raises:`". |

## 🟠 Never addressed — in qodo review but NOT in PR summary Part C (5 items)

| # | Item | Current state |
|---|------|---------------|
| 9 | `scripts/train.sh` recommends `pip install uv` | Still says `"Install with 'pip install uv'"` at `train.sh:29`. Rule 127825 forbids pip in tooling guidance. |
| 10 | `diff_aware_loss: bool` naming convention | Still `diff_aware_loss: bool` at `trainer.py:204`. Rule 127824 wants `is_/has_/can_/should_` prefix. |
| 11 | `DiffAwareSFTTrainer` doc claim / code mismatch | No `class DiffAwareSFTTrainer` exists in `diff_loss.py`; factory returns vanilla `trl.SFTTrainer`. README lines 30 & 122 still reference the class. |
| 12 | `_stratify_heldout_split` can empty train split | `n_heldout = max(1, ceil(...))` at `run_training_hpo.py:383` — with `N_tasks=1` yields `n_heldout=1`, leaving train empty. No clamp. |
| 13 | MLflow logs `assistant_only_loss=True` unconditionally | `trainer.py:338` sets config `"assistant_only_loss": not diff_aware_loss`; `trainer.py:605` logs run_param `"assistant_only_loss": True` hardcoded. Still mismatched. |

---

## Recommendation

Want me to:
1. **Restore the docstring regressions** (items 2 & 4) while still keeping griffe happy — e.g., `Raises:\n    Exception: Never; all errors are caught and logged.` format that parses as a valid exception-description pair.
2. **Fix the 5 unaddressed items** (9–13). Items 11 and 13 are the only real bugs; 9 is a tooling-docs rule, 10 is a naming convention, 12 is a small-dataset edge case.
3. **Just 11 + 13** (the actual correctness issues) and defer 9/10/12 as style items for a separate PR.

Which subset should I pick up?

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /Users/noahdolevelixir/.claude/plugins/cache/claude-plugins-official/superpowers/5.0.7/skills/subagent-driven-development

# Subagent-Driven Development

Execute plan by dispatching fresh subagent per task, with two-stage review after each: spec compliance review first, then code quality review.

**Why subagents:** You delegate tasks to specialized agents with isolated context. By precisely crafting their instructions and context, you ensure they stay focused and succeed at their task. They should never inherit your session's context or history — you construct exactly what they need. This also preserves your own context for coordination work.

**Core principle:** Fresh subagent per task + two-stage review (spec then quality) = high quality, fast iteration

## When to Use

```dot
digraph when_to_use {
    "Have implementation plan?" [shape=diamond];
    "Tasks mostly independent?" [shape=diamond];
    "Stay in this session?" [shape=diamond];
    "subagent-driven-development" [shape=box];
    "executing-plans" [shape=box];
    "Manual execution or brainstorm first" [shape=box];

    "Have implementation plan?" -> "Tasks mostly independent?" [label="yes"];
    "Have implementation plan?" -> "Manual execution or brainstorm first" [label="no"];
    "Tasks mostly independent?" -> "Stay in this session?" [label="yes"];
    "Tasks mostly independent?" -> "Manual execution or brainstorm first" [label="no - tightly coupled"];
    "Stay in this session?" -> "subagent-driven-development" [label="yes"];
    "Stay in this session?" -> "executing-plans" [label="no - parallel session"];
}
```

**vs. Executing Plans (parallel session):**
- Same session (no context switch)
- Fresh subagent per task (no context pollution)
- Two-stage review after each task: spec compliance first, then code quality
- Faster iteration (no human-in-loop between tasks)

## The Process

```dot
digraph process {
    rankdir=TB;

    subgraph cluster_per_task {
        label="Per Task";
        "Dispatch implementer subagent (./implementer-prompt.md)" [shape=box];
        "Implementer subagent asks questions?" [shape=diamond];
        "Answer questions, provide context" [shape=box];
        "Implementer subagent implements, tests, commits, self-reviews" [shape=box];
        "Dispatch spec reviewer subagent (./spec-reviewer-prompt.md)" [shape=box];
        "Spec reviewer subagent confirms code matches spec?" [shape=diamond];
        "Implementer subagent fixes spec gaps" [shape=box];
        "Dispatch code quality reviewer subagent (./code-quality-reviewer-prompt.md)" [shape=box];
        "Code quality reviewer subagent approves?" [shape=diamond];
        "Implementer subagent fixes quality issues" [shape=box];
        "Mark task complete in TodoWrite" [shape=box];
    }

    "Read plan, extract all tasks with full text, note context, create TodoWrite" [shape=box];
    "More tasks remain?" [shape=diamond];
    "Dispatch final code reviewer subagent for entire implementation" [shape=box];
    "Use superpowers:finishing-a-development-branch" [shape=box style=filled fillcolor=lightgreen];

    "Read plan, extract all tasks with full text, note context, create TodoWrite" -> "Dispatch implementer subagent (./implementer-prompt.md)";
    "Dispatch implementer subagent (./implementer-prompt.md)" -> "Implementer subagent asks questions?";
    "Implementer subagent asks questions?" -> "Answer questions, provide context" [label="yes"];
    "Answer questions, provide context" -> "Dispatch implementer subagent (./implementer-prompt.md)";
    "Implementer subagent asks questions?" -> "Implementer subagent implements, tests, commits, self-reviews" [label="no"];
    "Implementer subagent implements, tests, commits, self-reviews" -> "Dispatch spec reviewer subagent (./spec-reviewer-prompt.md)";
    "Dispatch spec reviewer subagent (./spec-reviewer-prompt.md)" -> "Spec reviewer subagent confirms code matches spec?";
    "Spec reviewer subagent confirms code matches spec?" -> "Implementer subagent fixes spec gaps" [label="no"];
    "Implementer subagent fixes spec gaps" -> "Dispatch spec reviewer subagent (./spec-reviewer-prompt.md)" [label="re-review"];
    "Spec reviewer subagent confirms code matches spec?" -> "Dispatch code quality reviewer subagent (./code-quality-reviewer-prompt.md)" [label="yes"];
    "Dispatch code quality reviewer subagent (./code-quality-reviewer-prompt.md)" -> "Code quality reviewer subagent approves?";
    "Code quality reviewer subagent approves?" -> "Implementer subagent fixes quality issues" [label="no"];
    "Implementer subagent fixes quality issues" -> "Dispatch code quality reviewer subagent (./code-quality-reviewer-prompt.md)" [label="re-review"];
    "Code quality reviewer subagent approves?" -> "Mark task complete in TodoWrite" [label="yes"];
    "Mark task complete in TodoWrite" -> "More tasks remain?";
    "More tasks remain?" -> "Dispatch implementer subagent (./implementer-prompt.md)" [label="yes"];
    "More tasks remain?" -> "Dispatch final code reviewer subagent for entire implementation" [label="no"];
    "Dispatch final code reviewer subagent for entire implementation" -> "Use superpowers:finishing-a-development-branch";
}
```

## Model Selection

Use the least powerful model that can handle each role to conserve cost and increase speed.

**Mechanical implementation tasks** (isolated functions, clear specs, 1-2 files): use a fast, cheap model. Most implementation tasks are mechanical when the plan is well-specified.

**Integration and judgment tasks** (multi-file coordination, pattern matching, debugging): use a standard model.

**Architecture, design, and review tasks**: use the most capable available model.

**Task complexity signals:**
- Touches 1-2 files with a complete spec → cheap model
- Touches multiple files with integration concerns → standard model
- Requires design judgment or broad codebase understanding → most capable model

## Handling Implementer Status

Implementer subagents report one of four statuses. Handle each appropriately:

**DONE:** Proceed to spec compliance review.

**DONE_WITH_CONCERNS:** The implementer completed the work but flagged doubts. Read the concerns before proceeding. If the concerns are about correctness or scope, address them before review. If they're observations (e.g., "this file is getting large"), note them and proceed to review.

**NEEDS_CONTEXT:** The implementer needs information that wasn't provided. Provide the missing context and re-dispatch.

**BLOCKED:** The implementer cannot complete the task. Assess the blocker:
1. If it's a context problem, provide more context and re-dispatch with the same model
2. If the task requires more reasoning, re-dispatch with a more capable model
3. If the task is too large, break it into smaller pieces
4. If the plan itself is wrong, escalate to the human

**Never** ignore an escalation or force the same model to retry without changes. If the implementer said it's stuck, something needs to change.

## Prompt Templates

- `./implementer-prompt.md` - Dispatch implementer subagent
- `./spec-reviewer-prompt.md` - Dispatch spec compliance reviewer subagent
- `./code-quality-reviewer-prompt.md` - Dispatch code quality reviewer subagent

## Example Workflow

```
You: I'm using Subagent-Driven Development to execute this plan.

[Read plan file once: docs/superpowers/plans/feature-plan.md]
[Extract all 5 tasks with full text and context]
[Create TodoWrite with all tasks]

Task 1: Hook installation script

[Get Task 1 text and context (already extracted)]
[Dispatch implementation subagent with full task text + context]

Implementer: "Before I begin - should the hook be installed at user or system level?"

You: "User level (~/.config/superpowers/hooks/)"

Implementer: "Got it. Implementing now..."
[Later] Implementer:
  - Implemented install-hook command
  - Added tests, 5/5 passing
  - Self-review: Found I missed --force flag, added it
  - Committed

[Dispatch spec compliance reviewer]
Spec reviewer: ✅ Spec compliant - all requirements met, nothing extra

[Get git SHAs, dispatch code quality reviewer]
Code reviewer: Strengths: Good test coverage, clean. Issues: None. Approved.

[Mark Task 1 complete]

Task 2: Recovery modes

[Get Task 2 text and context (already extracted)]
[Dispatch implementation subagent with full task text + context]

Implementer: [No questions, proceeds]
Implementer:
  - Added verify/repair modes
  - 8/8 tests passing
  - Self-review: All good
  - Committed

[Dispatch spec compliance reviewer]
Spec reviewer: ❌ Issues:
  - Missing: Progress reporting (spec says "report every 100 items")
  - Extra: Added --json flag (not requested)

[Implementer fixes issues]
Implementer: Removed --json flag, added progress reporting

[Spec reviewer reviews again]
Spec reviewer: ✅ Spec compliant now

[Dispatch code quality reviewer]
Code reviewer: Strengths: Solid. Issues (Important): Magic number (100)

[Implementer fixes]
Implementer: Extracted PROGRESS_INTERVAL constant

[Code reviewer reviews again]
Code reviewer: ✅ Approved

[Mark Task 2 complete]

...

[After all tasks]
[Dispatch final code-reviewer]
Final reviewer: All requirements met, ready to merge

Done!
```

## Advantages

**vs. Manual execution:**
- Subagents follow TDD naturally
- Fresh context per task (no confusion)
- Parallel-safe (subagents don't interfere)
- Subagent can ask questions (before AND during work)

**vs. Executing Plans:**
- Same session (no handoff)
- Continuous progress (no waiting)
- Review checkpoints automatic

**Efficiency gains:**
- No file reading overhead (controller provides full text)
- Controller curates exactly what context is needed
- Subagent gets complete information upfront
- Questions surfaced before work begins (not after)

**Quality gates:**
- Self-review catches issues before handoff
- Two-stage review: spec compliance, then code quality
- Review loops ensure fixes actually work
- Spec compliance prevents over/under-building
- Code quality ensures implementation is well-built

**Cost:**
- More subagent invocations (implementer + 2 reviewers per task)
- Controller does more prep work (extracting all tasks upfront)
- Review loops add iterations
- But catches issues early (cheaper than debugging later)

## Red Flags

**Never:**
- Start implementation on main/master branch without explicit user consent
- Skip reviews (spec compliance OR code quality)
- Proceed with unfixed issues
- Dispatch multiple implementation subagents in parallel (conflicts)
- Make subagent read plan file (provide full text instead)
- Skip scene-setting context (subagent needs to understand where task fits)
- Ignore subagent questions (answer before letting them proceed)
- Accept "close enough" on spec compliance (spec reviewer found issues = not done)
- Skip review loops (reviewer found issues = implementer fixes = review again)
- Let implementer self-review replace actual review (both are needed)
- **Start code quality review before spec compliance is ✅** (wrong order)
- Move to next task while either review has open issues

**If subagent asks questions:**
- Answer clearly and completely
- Provide additional context if needed
- Don't rush them into implementation

**If reviewer finds issues:**
- Implementer (same subagent) fixes them
- Reviewer reviews again
- Repeat until approved
- Don't skip the re-review

**If subagent fails task:**
- Dispatch fix subagent with specific instructions
- Don't try to fix manually (context pollution)

## Integration

**Required workflow skills:**
- **superpowers:using-git-worktrees** - REQUIRED: Set up isolated workspace before starting
- **superpowers:writing-plans** - Creates the plan this skill executes
- **superpowers:requesting-code-review** - Code review template for reviewer subagents
- **superpowers:finishing-a-development-branch** - Complete development after all tasks

**Subagents should use:**
- **superpowers:test-driven-development** - Subagents follow TDD for each task

**Alternative workflow:**
- **superpowers:executing-plans** - Use for parallel session instead of same-session execution
Acknowledging the skill. I'll read the three prompt templates and extract the 7 fix tasks before dispatching.

28	
29	    ## Your Job
30	
31	    Once you're clear on requirements:
32	    1. Implement exactly what the task specifies
33	    2. Write tests (following TDD if task says to)
34	    3. Verify implementation works
35	    4. Commit your work
36	    5. Self-review (see below)
37	    6. Report back
38	
39	    Work from: [directory]
40	
41	    **While you work:** If you encounter something unexpected or unclear, **ask questions**.
42	    It's always OK to pause and clarify. Don't guess or make assumptions.
43	
44	    ## Code Organization
45	
46	    You reason best about code you can hold in context at once, and your edits are more
47	    reliable when files are focused. Keep this in mind:
48	    - Follow the file structure defined in the plan
49	    - Each file should have one clear responsibility with a well-defined interface
50	    - If a file you're creating is growing beyond the plan's intent, stop and report
51	      it as DONE_WITH_CONCERNS — don't split files on your own without plan guidance
52	    - If an existing file you're modifying is already large or tangled, work carefully
53	      and note it as a concern in your report
54	    - In existing codebases, follow established patterns. Improve code you're touching
55	      the way a good developer would, but don't restructure things outside your task.
56	
57	    ## When You're in Over Your Head
58	
59	    It is always OK to stop and say "this is too hard for me." Bad work is worse than
60	    no work. You will not be penalized for escalating.
61	
62	    **STOP and escalate when:**
63	    - The task requires architectural decisions with multiple valid approaches
64	    - You need to understand code beyond what was provided and can't find clarity
65	    - You feel uncertain about whether your approach is correct
66	    - The task involves restructuring existing code in ways the plan didn't anticipate
67	    - You've been reading file after file trying to understand the system without progress
68	
69	    **How to escalate:** Report back with status BLOCKED or NEEDS_CONTEXT. Describe
70	    specifically what you're stuck on, what you've tried, and what kind of help you need.
71	    The controller can provide more context, re-dispatch with a more capable model,
72	    or break the task into smaller pieces.
73	
74	    ## Before Reporting Back: Self-Review
75	
76	    Review your work with fresh eyes. Ask yourself:
77	
78	    **Completeness:**
79	    - Did I fully implement everything in the spec?
80	    - Did I miss any requirements?
81	    - Are there edge cases I didn't handle?
82	
83	    **Quality:**
84	    - Is this my best work?
85	    - Are names clear and accurate (match what things do, not how they work)?
86	    - Is the code clean and maintainable?
87	
88	    **Discipline:**
89	    - Did I avoid overbuilding (YAGNI)?
90	    - Did I only build what was requested?
91	    - Did I follow existing patterns in the codebase?
92	
93	    **Testing:**
94	    - Do tests actually verify behavior (not just mock behavior)?
95	    - Did I follow TDD if required?
96	    - Are tests comprehensive?
97	
98	    If you find issues during self-review, fix them now before reporting.
99	
100	    ## Report Format
101	
102	    When done, report:
103	    - **Status:** DONE | DONE_WITH_CONCERNS | BLOCKED | NEEDS_CONTEXT
104	    - What you implemented (or what you attempted, if blocked)
105	    - What you tested and test results
106	    - Files changed
107	    - Self-review findings (if any)
108	    - Any issues or concerns
109	
110	    Use DONE_WITH_CONCERNS if you completed the work but have doubts about correctness.
111	    Use BLOCKED if you cannot complete the task. Use NEEDS_CONTEXT if you need
112	    information that wasn't provided. Never silently produce work you're unsure about.
113	```

# Implementer Subagent Prompt Template

Use this template when dispatching an implementer subagent.

```
Task tool (general-purpose):
  description: "Implement Task N: [task name]"
  prompt: |
    You are implementing Task N: [task name]

    ## Task Description

    [FULL TEXT of task from plan - paste it here, don't make subagent read file]

    ## Context

    [Scene-setting: where this fits, dependencies, architectural context]

    ## Before You Begin

    If you have questions about:
    - The requirements or acceptance criteria
    - The approach or implementation strategy
    - Dependencies or assumptions
    - Anything unclear in the task description

    **Ask them now.** Raise any concerns before starting work.

    ## Your Job

    Once you're clear on requirements:
    1. Implement exactly what the task specifies
    2. Write tests (following TDD if task says to)
    3. Verify implementation works
    4. Commit your work
    5. Self-review (see below)
    6. Report back

    Work from: [directory]

    **While you work:** If you encounter something unexpected or unclear, **ask questions**.
    It's always OK to pause and clarify. Don't guess or make assumptions.

    ## Code Organization

    You reason best about code you can hold in context at once, and your edits are more
    reliable when files are focused. Keep this in mind:
    - Follow the file structure defined in the plan
    - Each file should have one clear responsibility with a well-defined interface
    - If a file you're creating is growing beyond the plan's intent, stop and report
      it as DONE_WITH_CONCERNS — don't split files on your own without plan guidance
    - If an existing file you're modifying is already large or tangled, work carefully
      and note it as a concern in your report
    - In existing codebases, follow established patterns. Improve code you're touching
      the way a good developer would, but don't restructure things outside your task.

    ## When You're in Over Your Head

    It is always OK to stop and say "this is too hard for me." Bad work is worse than
    no work. You will not be penalized for escalating.

    **STOP and escalate when:**
    - The task requires architectural decisions with multiple valid approaches
    - You need to understand code beyond what was provided and can't find clarity
    - You feel uncertain about whether your approach is correct
    - The task involves restructuring existing code in ways the plan didn't anticipate
    - You've been reading file after file trying to understand the system without progress

    **How to escalate:** Report back with status BLOCKED or NEEDS_CONTEXT. Describe
    specifically what you're stuck on, what you've tried, and what kind of help you need.
    The controller can provide more context, re-dispatch with a more capable model,
    or break the task into smaller pieces.

    ## Before Reporting Back: Self-Review

    Review your work with fresh eyes. Ask yourself:

    **Completeness:**
    - Did I fully implement everything in the spec?
    - Did I miss any requirements?
    - Are there edge cases I didn't handle?

    **Quality:**
    - Is this my best work?
    - Are names clear and accurate (match what things do, not how they work)?
    - Is the code clean and maintainable?

    **Discipline:**
    - Did I avoid overbuilding (YAGNI)?
    - Did I only build what was requested?
    - Did I follow existing patterns in the codebase?

    **Testing:**
    - Do tests actually verify behavior (not just mock behavior)?
    - Did I follow TDD if required?
    - Are tests comprehensive?

    If you find issues during self-review, fix them now before reporting.

    ## Report Format

    When done, report:
    - **Status:** DONE | DONE_WITH_CONCERNS | BLOCKED | NEEDS_CONTEXT
    - What you implemented (or what you attempted, if blocked)
    - What you tested and test results
    - Files changed
    - Self-review findings (if any)
    - Any issues or concerns

    Use DONE_WITH_CONCERNS if you completed the work but have doubts about correctness.
    Use BLOCKED if you cannot complete the task. Use NEEDS_CONTEXT if you need
    information that wasn't provided. Never silently produce work you're unsure about.
```

# Spec Compliance Reviewer Prompt Template

Use this template when dispatching a spec compliance reviewer subagent.

**Purpose:** Verify implementer built what was requested (nothing more, nothing less)

```
Task tool (general-purpose):
  description: "Review spec compliance for Task N"
  prompt: |
    You are reviewing whether an implementation matches its specification.

    ## What Was Requested

    [FULL TEXT of task requirements]

    ## What Implementer Claims They Built

    [From implementer's report]

    ## CRITICAL: Do Not Trust the Report

    The implementer finished suspiciously quickly. Their report may be incomplete,
    inaccurate, or optimistic. You MUST verify everything independently.

    **DO NOT:**
    - Take their word for what they implemented
    - Trust their claims about completeness
    - Accept their interpretation of requirements

    **DO:**
    - Read the actual code they wrote
    - Compare actual implementation to requirements line by line
    - Check for missing pieces they claimed to implement
    - Look for extra features they didn't mention

    ## Your Job

    Read the implementation code and verify:

    **Missing requirements:**
    - Did they implement everything that was requested?
    - Are there requirements they skipped or missed?
    - Did they claim something works but didn't actually implement it?

    **Extra/unneeded work:**
    - Did they build things that weren't requested?
    - Did they over-engineer or add unnecessary features?
    - Did they add "nice to haves" that weren't in spec?

    **Misunderstandings:**
    - Did they interpret requirements differently than intended?
    - Did they solve the wrong problem?
    - Did they implement the right feature but wrong way?

    **Verify by reading code, not by trusting report.**

    Report:
    - ✅ Spec compliant (if everything matches after code inspection)
    - ❌ Issues found: [list specifically what's missing or extra, with file:line references]
```

content.content:
def build_diff_aware_sft_trainer(
    model: Any,
    args: Any,
    train_dataset: Any,
    *,
    peft_config: Any | None = None,
    processing_class: Any | None = None,
    changed_weight: float = 1.0,
    unchanged_weight: float = 0.3,
    tokenizer: Any | None = None,
) -> Any:
    """Build an SFTTrainer that uses diff-aware loss weighting.

    Constructs a :class:`DiffWeightedDataCollator` wrapping the default TRL
    completion-only collator, then instantiates ``trl.SFTTrainer`` with it.

    The ``tokenizer`` kwarg enables the hunk (line-level diff) path in the
    collator.  When ``None`` (default), the collator falls back to the legacy
    set-based path.  Task 5 threads the tokenizer through
    ``trainer.py::_construct_sft_trainer``; until then, callers may pass it
    explicitly here.

    All GPU imports are deferred to this function body (INFRA-05).

    Args:
        model: The language model (or PEFT-wrapped model).
        args: ``trl.SFTConfig`` training arguments.
        train_dataset: HuggingFace dataset with SFT examples.
        peft_config: Optional PEFT config for fresh LoRA init.
        processing_class: Tokenizer / processor for the SFTTrainer.
        changed_weight: Weight for changed / inserted tokens.
        unchanged_weight: Weight for unchanged (context) tokens.
        tokenizer: Optional tokenizer passed to :class:`DiffWeightedDataCollator`
            to enable the hunk path.  Falls back to ``processing_class`` if
            ``tokenizer`` is ``None`` and ``processing_class`` is provided.

    Returns:
        A configured ``trl.SFTTrainer`` instance.
    """
    from trl import (  # type: ignore[attr-defined]
        DataCollatorForCompletionOnlyLM,
        SFTTrainer,
    )

    resolved_tokenizer = tokenizer if tokenizer is not None else processing_class

    inner_collator = DataCollatorForCompletionOnlyLM(
        response_template="<|im_start|>assistant",
        tokenizer=resolved_tokenizer,
    )
    collator = DiffWeightedDataCollator(
        inner_collator,
        changed_weight=changed_weight,
        unchanged_weight=unchanged_weight,
        tokenizer=resolved_tokenizer,
    )

    return SFTTrainer(
        model=model,
        args=args,
        train_dataset=train_dataset,
        peft_config=peft_config,
        processing_class=processing_class,
        data_collator=collator,
    )

You are implementing Task A of a 5-task qodo-review cleanup for Rune PR #28.

Work from: /Users/noahdolevelixir/Code/rune
Branch: feat/training-upgrade (already checked out).
HEAD: 1ebf62b (pushed to origin).

## Task Description

Three small, independent fixes — all text-only, all in scope of the same commit.

### Fix 1 — restore `mlflow_log_params` structured docstring (qodo item 2)

File: `libs/model-training/src/model_training/training_common.py`
Function: `mlflow_log_params(params: dict[str, Any]) -> None` starting near line 49.

Current state: only has `Args:`. A previous commit (`6a26b54`) had added `Args:` + `Returns:` + `Raises:`, but a later doc-fix commit (`1ebf62b`) removed `Returns:` and `Raises:` because griffe's docstring parser was failing on `Raises:\n    None — description` (em-dash after `None` caused "Failed to get 'exception: description' pair").

You must restore the full `Args:` + `Returns:` + `Raises:` structure (qodo rule 127857 requires it) **while keeping `make docs-build` warning-free**.

Strategy: griffe's Google-style parser requires `<ExceptionName>: <description>` as the Raises-item format. Use `None: <description>` with a colon (not em-dash). Example that should parse:
```
Raises:
    None: All exceptions from mlflow.log_params are caught and logged at
        DEBUG level; MLflow unavailability never interrupts training.
```
If that still produces a griffe warning, try `Exception:` as the name (since the function does catch `Exception`, it's technically honest to document what's swallowed) — confirm by running `make docs-build` and checking for zero warnings.

Also include a `Returns:` section: `Returns:\n    None.` — one line.

### Fix 2 — restore `pairs_to_chat_messages` `Raises:` section (qodo item 4)

File: `libs/model-training/src/model_training/d2l_data.py`
Function: `pairs_to_chat_messages` starting near line 935.

Current state: the `Returns:` section is intact prose; no `Raises:` section.

Re-add a `Raises:` section at the end of the docstring (after `Returns:`). The function does not raise — so mirror the format you settled on in Fix 1 (griffe-parseable + qodo-compliant). Keep wording factual ("No exceptions are raised — invalid input fields fall back to empty-string / zero defaults.").

### Fix 3 — `scripts/train.sh` uv bootstrap message (qodo item 9)

File: `scripts/train.sh`, near line 28-31:
```
if ! command -v uv >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    echo "train.sh: 'uv' not found on PATH. Install with 'pip install uv' or" \
         "see https://docs.astral.sh/uv/." >&2
    exit 127
```

Qodo rule 127825 forbids recommending `pip` for Python tooling. Change the message so it points at the official uv installer (curl-based) and docs URL, **without mentioning pip**. Astral's canonical one-liner is:
```
curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh
```
Keep the existing multi-line `echo ... \` shell syntax structure and the `exit 127` unchanged. Update the wording only.

## Context

- PR #28 summary: `instructions/2026-04-23-pr-28-summary.md` — read Part C qodo items 2, 4 for background.
- Qodo rule links (for reference, do not fetch): 127857 (structured docstrings), 127825 (uv-only tooling guidance).
- The griffe warnings that motivated the prior removal are documented in git log for commit `1ebf62b`.
- After changes, `make docs-build` must run clean (0 warnings, 0 errors).
- `uv run ruff check` and `uv run ruff format --check` must pass.
- No tests to add — these are doc/text fixes.

## Before You Begin

If anything above is ambiguous — especially the exact griffe-parseable format for `Raises:` — please ask. It's better to clarify than guess: the wrong format silently reintroduces docs-build warnings.

## Your Job

1. Read the three target files.
2. Apply the three fixes.
3. Run `make docs-build` — must show 0 WARNING / 0 ERROR lines from griffe.
4. Run `uv run ruff check` and `uv run ruff format --check` — must pass.
5. Run `uv run pytest libs/model-training/tests/ -q` (just the model-training tests, fast) to make sure no docstring changes broke imports.
6. Commit all three fixes in ONE commit with a message like `fix(pr28-review): restore structured docstrings + drop pip from train.sh`. Reference the qodo item numbers (2, 4, 9) in the body.
7. Self-review before reporting.
8. Report with Status: DONE | DONE_WITH_CONCERNS | BLOCKED | NEEDS_CONTEXT.

Do NOT push. The controller will push after all tasks land.

## Self-review checklist before reporting

- [ ] All three fixes applied.
- [ ] `make docs-build` shows zero WARNING / ERROR lines.
- [ ] `uv run ruff check` passes.
- [ ] `uv run ruff format --check` passes.
- [ ] Tests for model-training pass.
- [ ] One commit with clear message referencing items 2/4/9.
- [ ] `mkdocs.yml` and `docs/components-overview.md` regeneration artifacts are NOT committed (they drift every build).

content.text:

**What was applied:**

**Fix 1** (`training_common.py`, line 55) — `mlflow_log_params`: restored `Returns: None.` and `Raises: Exception: ...` sections. Used `Exception:` as the Raises label (griffe requires `<Name>: description` format; `Exception` is also honest since the function catches `Exception`).

**Fix 2** (`d2l_data.py`, ~line 981) — `pairs_to_chat_messages`: appended a `Raises:` section after `Returns:` using the same `Exception:` label, documenting that no exceptions propagate.

**Fix 3** (`scripts/train.sh`, line 29-31) — replaced `pip install uv` with `curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh` across the multi-line echo, pip not mentioned.

## Task Description

Three small, independent fixes — all text-only, all in scope of the same commit.

### Fix 1 — restore `mlflow_log_params` structured docstring (qodo item 2)

File: `libs/model-training/src/model_training/training_common.py`
Function: `mlflow_log_params(params: dict[str, Any]) -> None` starting near line 49.

Current state: only has `Args:`. A previous commit (`6a26b54`) had added `Args:` + `Returns:` + `Raises:`, but a later doc-fix commit (`1ebf62b`) removed `Returns:` and `Raises:` because griffe's docstring parser was failing on `Raises:\n    None — description` (em-dash after `None` caused "Failed to get 'exception: description' pair").

You must restore the full `Args:` + `Returns:` + `Raises:` structure (qodo rule 127857 requires it) **while keeping `make docs-build` warning-free**.

Strategy: griffe's Google-style parser requires `<ExceptionName>: <description>` as the Raises-item format. Use `None: <description>` with a colon (not em-dash). Example that should parse:
```
Raises:
    None: All exceptions from mlflow.log_params are caught and logged at
        DEBUG level; MLflow unavailability never interrupts training.
```
If that still produces a griffe warning, try `Exception:` as the name (since the function does catch `Exception`, it's technically honest to document what's swallowed) — confirm by running `make docs-build` and checking for zero warnings.

Also include a `Returns:` section: `Returns:\n    None.` — one line.

### Fix 2 — restore `pairs_to_chat_messages` `Raises:` section (qodo item 4)

File: `libs/model-training/src/model_training/d2l_data.py`
Function: `pairs_to_chat_messages` starting near line 935.

Current state: the `Returns:` section is intact prose; no `Raises:` section.

Re-add a `Raises:` section at the end of the docstring (after `Returns:`). The function does not raise — so mirror the format you settled on in Fix 1 (griffe-parseable + qodo-compliant). Keep wording factual ("No exceptions are raised — invalid input fields fall back to empty-string / zero defaults.").

### Fix 3 — `scripts/train.sh` uv bootstrap message (qodo item 9)

File: `scripts/train.sh`, near line 28-31:
```
if ! command -v uv >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    echo "train.sh: 'uv' not found on PATH. Install with 'pip install uv' or" \
         "see https://docs.astral.sh/uv/." >&2
    exit 127
```

Qodo rule 127825 forbids recommending `pip` for Python tooling. Change the message so it points at the official uv installer (curl-based) and docs URL, **without mentioning pip**. Astral's canonical one-liner is:
```
curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh
```
Keep the existing multi-line `echo ... \` shell syntax structure and the `exit 127` unchanged. Update the wording only.

- PR #28 summary: `instructions/2026-04-23-pr-28-summary.md` — read Part C qodo items 2, 4 for background.
- Qodo rule links (for reference, do not fetch): 127857 (structured docstrings), 127825 (uv-only tooling guidance).

input.prompt:
Review the code quality of commit `34614c7` on branch `feat/training-upgrade` of `/Users/noahdolevelixir/Code/rune`. This commit addresses qodo PR #28 review items 2, 4, 9.

## What Was Implemented

Three text/doc fixes in one commit:

1. **`libs/model-training/src/model_training/training_common.py`** — `mlflow_log_params` docstring: restored `Returns:` and `Raises:` sections that were removed in commit `1ebf62b` (which removed them to fix griffe docs-build warnings). The new format uses `Exception: <description>` (colon + real-ish exception name) so griffe's Google-style parser can parse it while still satisfying qodo rule 127857 (structured docstrings on public callables).

2. **`libs/model-training/src/model_training/d2l_data.py`** — `pairs_to_chat_messages` docstring: appended a `Raises:` section using the same griffe-parseable `Exception: <desc>` format. The function never raises (invalid fields fall back to empty strings / zero defaults) — the section is a qodo-required formality.

3. **`scripts/train.sh`** — replaced the `pip install uv` error message with the official curl-based uv installer one-liner (qodo rule 127825 forbids recommending pip for Python tooling).

## What to Review

Focus on:
- Is the docstring wording accurate and clear? The two functions don't actually raise anything caller-visible. Is `Exception:` an honest label here, or does it misrepresent behavior to downstream API doc consumers who read the generated MkDocs site?
- Is the `train.sh` error message still useful when uv is missing? Does the user get a clear path to install?
- Any regression risk — the removed em-dash format was failing griffe; is the colon format robust across all three files?
- Does the commit stay focused (no scope creep, no unrelated edits)?

## Review Context

- BASE_SHA: `1ebf62b` (commit before Task A)
- HEAD_SHA: `34614c7` (Task A commit)
- Verification already confirmed by spec reviewer: `make docs-build` runs clean, `ruff check` passes, 370 model-training tests pass.

## Report

Strengths, Issues (Critical/Important/Minor), Assessment. Keep under 400 words.

content.text:
---

**Strengths**

- Scope is clean: exactly 3 files, 15 lines, zero collateral changes.
- The `training_common.py` `Raises:` text accurately mirrors the try/except behavior visible two lines below the docstring — easy for a reader to verify.
- Dropping `pip install uv` aligns with CLAUDE.md convention and the official installer URL is correct (`astral.sh/uv/install.sh`).
- The commit message enumerates each qodo item with a rationale — good traceability.

---

**Issues**

Important — `Exception:` is a semantically false label in `pairs_to_chat_messages`

The docstring reads:

> `Exception: No exceptions are raised — invalid input fields fall back to empty-string / zero defaults.`

`Exception` in a griffe Google-style `Raises:` block is parsed as "the type that may be raised." Using it to mean "nothing is raised" is self-contradictory. Downstream API doc consumers reading the MkDocs output will see a `Raises` table with entry `Exception` and infer the function can throw. The prose clarifies it does not, but the structure contradicts the prose.

The better resolution is one of:
- Drop the `Raises:` section entirely from `pairs_to_chat_messages` — the function does not raise, no griffe warning is produced by omitting the section, and the graceful-fallback behavior is already documented in the `Returns:` block.
- If qodo rule 127857 mandates the section even on non-raising functions, use `None: No exceptions are raised.` — griffe will still parse it as a valid entry and the label no longer implies a live exception type.

Minor — `Returns: None.` in `mlflow_log_params`

`-> None` is already in the signature. A one-word `Returns:` section adds no information and makes the docstring slightly longer for readers and tooling to parse. Not wrong, but adds noise.

Minor — `train.sh` message mixes quoting styles

The single-quoted curl command embedded in a double-quoted echo with backslash-continuation is correct bash but visually fragile. A heredoc or a single `printf` would be more readable. Low risk since the script is an invocation wrapper, not a library.

---

**Assessment**

Two of the three changes are clean and correct. The third (`pairs_to_chat_messages` `Raises:` section) has an accuracy problem: the label `Exception` actively misleads doc readers into thinking the function can raise. Given the MkDocs output is a public artifact, this is worth fixing before merging. The recommended fix is dropping the section entirely — nothing in the `Returns:` block needs to change.

Relevant files:
- `/Users/noahdolevelixir/Code/rune/libs/model-training/src/model_training/d2l_data.py` (lines 984-986)
- `/Users/noahdolevelixir/Code/rune/libs/model-training/src/model_training/training_common.py` (lines 59-65)
- `/Users/noahdolevelixir/Code/rune/scripts/train.sh` (lines 28-32)

You are fixing an Important issue flagged during code review of commit `34614c7` on branch `feat/training-upgrade` of `/Users/noahdolevelixir/Code/rune`.

## Issue

The `Raises:` sections in commit `34614c7` used `Exception: <description>` as the entry label. The code reviewer correctly observed that this is semantically false — griffe / MkDocs parse `Exception` as "this function can raise Exception," but neither `mlflow_log_params` nor `pairs_to_chat_messages` actually raises any exception to the caller. The rendered API docs will mislead readers.

Affected docstrings:
- `libs/model-training/src/model_training/training_common.py::mlflow_log_params` (lines ~62-65)
- `libs/model-training/src/model_training/d2l_data.py::pairs_to_chat_messages` (lines ~984-986)

## The Constraint

Earlier work established that griffe's Google-style parser produces `WARNING -  griffe: ... Failed to get 'exception: description' pair from '...'` when the `Raises:` body does not start with `<Name>: <description>` (colon required, em-dash fails). That was the original reason the section was rewritten.

Qodo rule 127857 (structured docstrings) *suggested* `None` as the label in its agent prompt for item 4 ("even when no intended exceptions, the rule requires an explicit `Raises:` section (e.g., `None`)").

## Your Task

Change the `Raises:` body in both functions from `Exception: ...` to `None: ...` (keep the colon separator so griffe parses the pair). This satisfies the reviewer (semantically accurate — `None` clearly means "no exceptions raised"), satisfies qodo (explicit section present), and — hypothesis to verify — should satisfy griffe since the `<Name>: <description>` shape is preserved.

**Verification required:** after editing, run `make docs-build 2>&1 | grep -E "WARNING|ERROR" | head -20`. The output MUST be empty (zero WARNING/ERROR lines from griffe).

If `None:` produces a griffe warning (for example if griffe rejects `None` as an invalid exception name), try these fallbacks in order:
1. `NoException: ...` — nonsense name but valid identifier; ugly.
2. Drop the `Raises:` section entirely from both functions. This will leave the docstrings with `Args:` + `Returns:` only. Note in your report that qodo rule 127857 may flag this — acceptable tradeoff since the Raises section cannot be simultaneously honest and griffe-parseable.

Whichever approach ends up working, commit both fixes together with a clean message describing the rationale. Commit title suggestion:
```
fix(pr28-review): use None: label for no-raise Raises sections
```

Amend-commit onto `34614c7` is fine (same commit is still unpushed). Or create a follow-up fixup commit — controller will rebase if needed.

## Self-review checklist

- [ ] Both docstrings updated consistently.
- [ ] `make docs-build` output has zero griffe WARNING / ERROR lines.
- [ ] `uv run ruff check` + `uv run ruff format --check` pass.
- [ ] If you fell back to dropping `Raises:`, note the qodo tradeoff clearly in commit message + report.
- [ ] Commit focused; no unrelated changes.

Report:
- **Status:** DONE | DONE_WITH_CONCERNS | BLOCKED
- Which approach worked (`None:` / `NoException:` / dropped section).
- Verification output (the `grep WARNING|ERROR` result).
- Commit SHA.

Do NOT push.

**Approach:** `None:` — worked first try, no fallback needed.

**Verification:** `make docs-build 2>&1 | grep -E "WARNING|ERROR" | head -20` produced zero output. griffe accepted `None:` as a valid `<Name>: <description>` pair.

**Lint:** `ruff check` and `ruff format --check` both passed on both files.

**Commit:** `b6b62fd` — two files changed, 2 insertions / 2 deletions, no unrelated changes.

## Task Description

Two small, independent fixes in the same subsystem — commit together.

### Fix 1 — `_stratify_heldout_split` train-set empty guard (qodo item 12)

File: `scripts/optimization/run_training_hpo.py`
Function: `_stratify_heldout_split(pairs, *, fraction, strategy, seed=42)` — around line 352.

Current offending logic (line ~383):
```python
n_heldout = max(1, math.ceil(len(task_ids) * fraction)) if task_ids else 0
```

With `fraction=0.2` and only 1 task, this yields `n_heldout=1` — the split produces an empty train set, and downstream training runs zero steps or crashes. Qodo's suggested fix:
- If `fraction <= 0`, return `(pairs, [])`.
- Otherwise clamp `n_heldout` to at most `len(task_ids) - 1` when `len(task_ids) > 1`.
- If the configured split would leave train empty (e.g., `len(task_ids) == 1`), raise a clear `ValueError` so the caller gets a legible error instead of a silent zero-step run.

Implement that. Use `ValueError` with a message like:
```
"Heldout split would leave train set empty: got N_tasks=1; "
"at least 2 tasks are required when fraction > 0."
```

### Fix 2 — MLflow `assistant_only_loss` run-param mismatch (qodo item 13)

File: `libs/model-training/src/model_training/trainer.py`
Location: two spots.

**Truth site (SFTConfig construction, ~line 338):**
```python
"assistant_only_loss": not diff_aware_loss,
```
This is correct — when diff-aware loss is on, the custom collator handles masking so SFTConfig's built-in `assistant_only_loss` must be off.

**Logging site (run_params dict, ~line 605):**
```python
"assistant_only_loss": True,
```
This hardcodes `True`, which lies in MLflow for any diff-aware trial. Fix by logging the actual effective value:
```python
"assistant_only_loss": not diff_aware_loss,
```

## Tests

Add/update tests for both fixes.

### Fix 1 tests
Find the existing test file for `_stratify_heldout_split` (should be under `tests/optimization/` or `scripts/optimization/tests/` or similar — use `grep -r "stratify_heldout_split" tests/ scripts/` to locate). Add:
- Test case: `fraction=0` returns `(pairs, [])` unchanged.
- Test case: `len(task_ids)==1, fraction>0` raises `ValueError` with a message mentioning the empty-train condition.
- Test case: `len(task_ids)==2, fraction=0.5` yields 1 heldout task, 1 train task (verifying clamp is unnecessary here).
- Test case: `len(task_ids)==5, fraction=0.6` would naively yield `ceil(3)=3` heldout; clamp must keep train non-empty — expect `n_heldout==min(3, 4)=3` with 2 train (i.e., the clamp doesn't fire when train would still have >=1 task). Then verify with `fraction=0.9, N=2` → clamp fires, `n_heldout=1`, 1 train.

### Fix 2 tests
Find the test file for `train_qlora` / `_build_run_params` (or whatever function builds the MLflow run_params dict). If there's a unit test that already asserts on the dict's keys/values, extend it. If no test exercises that dict directly, add one that:
- Constructs run_params in both modes (`diff_aware_loss=True` and `diff_aware_loss=False`).
- Asserts `run_params["assistant_only_loss"] == (not diff_aware_loss)` in both cases.

If the run_params construction is inline inside `train_qlora` rather than a separable helper, either:
(a) lightly refactor it into a `_build_run_params` helper that takes `diff_aware_loss` and returns the dict, then test that helper. Keep the refactor minimal — just extraction, no behavior change.
(b) or test via a narrower integration harness that calls enough of `train_qlora` to observe the dict.

Option (a) is preferred if the existing code is amenable. If `train_qlora` is tightly coupled (GPU imports, mlflow.start_run inline), use option (b) or mock mlflow.

## Acceptance

1. Both fixes applied.
2. New tests exist and pass.
3. Full model-training + optimization test suite passes: `uv run pytest libs/model-training/tests/ scripts/optimization/ -q` (adjust path if optimization tests live elsewhere — use `uv run pytest` from repo root to be safe but scope narrower if possible for speed).
4. `uv run ruff check` + `uv run ruff format --check` pass.
5. `make docs-build` still warning-free.
6. One commit with clear message referencing qodo items 12 and 13. Title suggestion: `fix(pr28-review): clamp heldout split + fix assistant_only_loss MLflow log`.
7. No scope creep — touch only the necessary files.

## Before You Begin

If the run_params construction in `trainer.py` is tangled or the existing heldout-split test layout is unclear, ask. Don't refactor broadly. These are surgical fixes.

Review code quality of commit `faec41b` on branch `feat/training-upgrade` of `/Users/noahdolevelixir/Code/rune`. This addresses qodo PR #28 review items 12 and 13.

## What Was Implemented

Two correctness fixes committed together.

**Fix 1 — `scripts/optimization/run_training_hpo.py::_stratify_heldout_split`** — added three guards in this order: (a) early `return (list(pairs), [])` when `fraction <= 0`; (b) raise `ValueError` with message mentioning `N_tasks=1` when `len(task_ids) == 1 and fraction > 0`; (c) clamp `n_heldout = min(max(1, ceil(...)), n_tasks - 1)` so train is never empty when N≥2. Five new tests in `scripts/optimization/tests/test_training_hpo.py` cover each branch.

**Fix 2 — `libs/model-training/src/model_training/trainer.py`** — extracted the inline MLflow `run_params` dict into a module-level helper `_build_run_params(...)` and corrected `assistant_only_loss` from hardcoded `True` to `not diff_aware_loss` (matching the SFTConfig truth site a few hundred lines earlier). Two new tests in `libs/model-training/tests/test_trainer_mlflow.py` verify both modes.

## Spec Review Already Passed

A spec compliance reviewer verified the `_build_run_params` extraction is pure — every key/value preserved, call site passes locals by identical names, and the only intentional value change is the `assistant_only_loss` correction.

## Review Focus

Focus your review on code quality, not spec compliance:
- Helper placement: is `_build_run_params` in the right place in the file? Private (underscore) name appropriate?
- Helper signature: reasonable parameter list, or does it take so many args that it suggests a different design?
- Test quality: are the new tests checking real behavior (not just mocking the dict back at itself)?
- Error message quality: is the `ValueError` message for the 1-task case useful for a real operator (gives actionable info about what to change)?
- Any subtle issues introduced by the clamp math — e.g., off-by-one with fraction=1.0 + N=2.

BASE_SHA: `b6b62fd`
HEAD_SHA: `faec41b`

## Report

Strengths, Issues (Critical/Important/Minor), Assessment. Under 400 words.

content.text:
I have enough context. Here is the review.

---

**Strengths**

- `_build_run_params` is correctly placed immediately before `_build_sft_config` and `train_qlora` in the module, adjacent to the context it serves. Underscore prefix is appropriate — it is a module-internal helper with no reason to be public API.
- Keyword-only signature (`*`) prevents positional-argument mismatches at the 22-argument call site. Given the call site passes every argument by name anyway, the design is correct.
- The `assistant_only_loss` correction (`not diff_aware_loss`) is the right inversion and the docstring explains the semantic link to SFTConfig clearly.
- The `fraction <= 0` early-return is placed before the shuffle, which avoids unnecessary randomness side effects.
- All five HPO tests assert real output properties (task ID disjointness, counts, raises) rather than just mocking the function's own internals.

---

**Issues**

**Important — clamp edge case: `fraction=1.0` with `N=2` produces misleading behavior**

With `n_tasks=2` and `fraction=1.0`: `ceil(2 * 1.0) = 2`, clamped to `n_tasks - 1 = 1`. Result: 1 heldout, 1 train. This is technically safe (train is non-empty), but `fraction=1.0` semantically means "hold out everything," so silently clamping to 50/50 is a footgun. The existing ValueError for `N=1` demonstrates the intent to fail loudly on degenerate input — the same principle should apply here. No test covers `fraction=1.0` with `N >= 2`, so this silent behavior is undetected.

Recommendation: add a guard `if math.ceil(n_tasks * fraction) >= n_tasks: raise ValueError(...)` before the clamp, or at minimum add a test documenting the clamping behavior at `fraction=1.0`.

**Important — `_build_run_params` has 22 parameters, coupling the signature to every future training hyperparameter**

This is the structural tension introduced by extracting the dict: the helper's signature is now a shadow of `train_qlora`'s signature. Adding any new training param requires touching three sites (train_qlora params, _build_run_params params, call site). A `**kwargs: object` dict pass-through, or a small dataclass/TypedDict grouping the params that feed both `_build_run_params` and `_build_sft_config`, would eliminate this coupling. Not blocking, but worth flagging as a scaling concern.

**Minor — the single-task test title does not match what it tests**

`test_heldout_split_single_task_raises` says "One task + fraction>0" in its docstring but passes pairs with two rows sharing one task ID (`_mkpair("only", 0)`, `_mkpair("only", 1)`). The test is correct — `task_ids` will have length 1 — but the docstring omits the detail that it is two *pairs* from one task, which may confuse a future reader auditing why 2 pairs produce `N_tasks=1`.

**Minor — MLflow test duplication**

`test_run_params_assistant_only_loss_without_diff_aware` and `test_run_params_assistant_only_loss_with_diff_aware` are near-identical: 22 identical kwargs, one boolean flipped. A single parametrized test (`@pytest.mark.parametrize("diff_aware_loss,expected", [(False, True), (True, False)])`) would be shorter and less brittle if the signature grows.

---

**Assessment**

Both fixes are correct and the tests cover the real behavioral branches. The `assistant_only_loss` bug fix is the most consequential change and is verified properly. The clamp math at `fraction=1.0` with `N >= 2` is a latent silent-footgun that should either be tested-and-documented or guarded with a ValueError matching the style of the existing 1-task guard. The 22-parameter helper is a design smell inherited from the inline dict, not introduced by this PR, but worth tracking. No blockers; the `fraction=1.0` gap is the one issue worth addressing before this merges.

input.prompt:
You are addressing code-quality review findings on commit `faec41b` on branch `feat/training-upgrade` of `/Users/noahdolevelixir/Code/rune`. Work from that directory.

The reviewer approved the core fixes for qodo items 12 and 13 but raised one Important blocker and two Minor cleanups.

## Important — add ValueError guard for full-fraction heldout

**File:** `scripts/optimization/run_training_hpo.py` in `_stratify_heldout_split`.

Current state: with `n_tasks=2` and `fraction=1.0`, `ceil(2 * 1.0) = 2`, then silently clamped to `n_tasks - 1 = 1`. This gives 1 heldout, 1 train — technically safe but `fraction=1.0` semantically means "hold out everything", so silent 50/50 is a footgun. The existing single-task guard (which raises `ValueError` on degenerate input) establishes the project's preference for loud failure.

**Change:** after computing the raw `ceil(n_tasks * fraction)` but before clamping, raise `ValueError` when the raw value `>= n_tasks`. Message example:

```
"Heldout split would leave train set empty: fraction=1.0 with N_tasks=2 "
"would hold out all tasks. Reduce fraction so at least one task remains "
"for training."
```

Add a test: `n_tasks=2, fraction=1.0` must raise `ValueError` with `match="would leave train set empty"` (or similar key phrase; align with the 1-task test's match pattern for consistency).

Keep the existing 1-task `ValueError` and clamp branch untouched — they handle the other degenerate cases.

## Minor 1 — clarify `test_heldout_split_single_task_raises` docstring

**File:** `scripts/optimization/tests/test_training_hpo.py`

The test constructs two pairs that share one task_id (`_mkpair("only", 0)` and `_mkpair("only", 1)`). Docstring currently says "One task + fraction>0" which is accurate in terms of `N_tasks` but omits that it's two pairs from the same task. Update the docstring to something like:

> "Two pairs sharing one task_id (N_tasks=1) with fraction>0 raises ValueError."

Single-line fix. Don't rewrite the test body.

## Minor 2 — parametrize the MLflow run_params tests

**File:** `libs/model-training/tests/test_trainer_mlflow.py`

The two tests `test_run_params_assistant_only_loss_without_diff_aware` and `test_run_params_assistant_only_loss_with_diff_aware` are near-identical (22 kwargs, one flipped bool). Replace them with a single `@pytest.mark.parametrize("diff_aware_loss,expected_assistant_only", [(False, True), (True, False)])` test covering both cases. Keep the assertion identical to what each individual test asserted.

## Acceptance

1. New guard + new test for fraction=1.0 / full heldout case.
2. Single-task test docstring clarified.
3. MLflow run_params tests parametrized (two tests collapse into one parametrized test).
4. `uv run pytest scripts/optimization/tests/test_training_hpo.py libs/model-training/tests/test_trainer_mlflow.py -q` passes (and the parametrized test reports 2 passes, not 1).
5. `uv run ruff check` + `uv run ruff format --check` clean.
6. `make docs-build` still warning-free (should be unaffected).
7. One commit amending or following up on `faec41b`. Either (a) `git commit --amend --no-edit` with rebasing of the follow-up changes, or (b) a new fixup commit like `fix(pr28-review): add full-fraction heldout guard + tidy tests`. Option (b) is safer since it avoids losing the original commit message.

## Self-Review Before Reporting

- Re-read the new ValueError message — does a real HPO operator know what to change?
- Parametrize idiom: is the parameter tuple accurate and the test name still meaningful?
- No scope creep — don't refactor `_build_run_params` or the full stratification function.

## Report

- Status: DONE | DONE_WITH_CONCERNS | BLOCKED
- Commit SHA
- Test pass count for the two affected test files (from `-q` output).
- Any concerns.

Do NOT push. Controller handles push.

**Status: DONE**

**Commit:** `10322cb`

**Test results:** 29 passed in 3.65s across the two files. The parametrized MLflow test reports 2 passes (one per `(False, True)` / `(True, False)` tuple), not 1.

**Changes made:**

1. **ValueError guard** (`run_training_hpo.py` lines 390–397): compute `raw_heldout = ceil(n_tasks * fraction)` first, raise `ValueError` with `"would leave train set empty"` when `raw_heldout >= n_tasks`. The clamp line still exists and covers the `max(1, ...)` lower-bound edge case, but the upper-bound silent clamp is now a loud error.

2. **Single-task docstring** (`test_training_hpo.py`): updated to "Two pairs sharing one task_id (N_tasks=1) with fraction>0 raises ValueError."

3. **New test `test_heldout_split_full_fraction_raises`**: `n_tasks=2, fraction=1.0` → `ValueError` matching `"would leave train set empty"`.

4. **`test_heldout_split_clamp_fires_for_high_fraction` updated**: `fraction=0.9` with 2 tasks now also hits the guard (`ceil(1.8)=2 >= 2`), so the test was updated to expect the same `ValueError` rather than a silent clamp result. This is semantically correct — high fractions that exhaust all tasks are now uniformly rejected.

5. **MLflow tests parametrized**: two 22-kwarg tests collapsed into one `@pytest.mark.parametrize("diff_aware_loss,expected_assistant_only", [(False, True), (True, False)])` test.

=== current diff_loss.py build_diff_aware_sft_trainer ===
def build_diff_aware_sft_trainer(
    model: Any,
    args: Any,
    train_dataset: Any,
    *,
    peft_config: Any | None = None,
    processing_class: Any | None = None,
    changed_weight: float = 1.0,
    unchanged_weight: float = 0.3,
    tokenizer: Any | None = None,
) -> Any:
    """Build an SFTTrainer that uses diff-aware loss weighting.

    Constructs a :class:`DiffWeightedDataCollator` wrapping the default TRL
    completion-only collator, then instantiates ``trl.SFTTrainer`` with it.

    The ``tokenizer`` kwarg enables the hunk (line-level diff) path in the
    collator.  When ``None`` (default), the collator falls back to the legacy
    set-based path.  Task 5 threads the tokenizer through
    ``trainer.py::_construct_sft_trainer``; until then, callers may pass it
    explicitly here.

    All GPU imports are deferred to this function body (INFRA-05).

    Args:
        model: The language model (or PEFT-wrapped model).
        args: ``trl.SFTConfig`` training arguments.
        train_dataset: HuggingFace dataset with SFT examples.
        peft_config: Optional PEFT config for fresh LoRA init.
        processing_class: Tokenizer / processor for the SFTTrainer.
        changed_weight: Weight for changed / inserted tokens.

def build_diff_aware_sft_trainer(
    model: Any,
    args: Any,
    train_dataset: Any,
    *,

    processing_class: Any | None = None,
    changed_weight: float = 1.0,

    tokenizer: Any | None = None,
) -> Any:
    """Build an SFTTrainer that uses diff-aware loss weighting.

    Constructs a :class:`DiffWeightedDataCollator` wrapping the default TRL
    completion-only collator, then instantiates ``trl.SFTTrainer`` with it.

    The ``tokenizer`` kwarg enables the hunk (line-level diff) path in the
    collator.  When ``None`` (default), the collator falls back to the legacy
    set-based path.  Task 5 threads the tokenizer through
    ``trainer.py::_construct_sft_trainer``; until then, callers may pass it
    explicitly here.

    All GPU imports are deferred to this function body (INFRA-05).

        args: ``trl.SFTConfig`` training arguments.

            to enable the hunk path.  Falls back to ``processing_class`` if
            ``tokenizer`` is ``None`` and ``processing_class`` is provided.

    Returns:
        A configured ``trl.SFTTrainer`` instance.
    """
    from trl import (  # type: ignore[attr-defined]
        DataCollatorForCompletionOnlyLM,
        SFTTrainer,
    )

    resolved_tokenizer = tokenizer if tokenizer is not None else processing_class

    inner_collator = DataCollatorForCompletionOnlyLM(
        response_template="<|im_start|>assistant",
        tokenizer=resolved_tokenizer,
    )
    collator = DiffWeightedDataCollator(
        inner_collator,
        changed_weight=changed_weight,
        unchanged_weight=unchanged_weight,
        tokenizer=resolved_tokenizer,
    )

    return SFTTrainer(
        model=model,
        args=args,

input.prompt:
You are implementing Task C of the qodo PR #28 cleanup. Work from `/Users/noahdolevelixir/Code/rune`. Branch `feat/training-upgrade` is checked out. Current HEAD: `10322cb`.

## Task Description (qodo item 11)

The README and various docs state that `diff_aware_loss=True` "swaps in a `DiffAwareSFTTrainer`" that biases per-token loss toward revision diff tokens. The reality: the factory `build_diff_aware_sft_trainer` in `libs/model-training/src/model_training/diff_loss.py` returns a vanilla `trl.SFTTrainer`. The `DiffWeightedDataCollator` attaches a `loss_weights` tensor to each batch, but no trainer consumes it — so the hunk-weighted loss is COMPUTED AND DISCARDED. The feature is broken at the semantic level.

Qodo's agent prompt offered two resolution options:
1. Implement an actual `DiffAwareSFTTrainer` that consumes `loss_weights` in its loss function.
2. Update docs to describe the current vanilla mechanism.

The user has chosen **option 1** — implement the actual class. This is a real code fix, not a doc fix.

## Architecture You Must Implement

Add to `libs/model-training/src/model_training/diff_loss.py`:

```python
class DiffAwareSFTTrainer(SFTTrainer):
    """SFTTrainer that applies per-token loss weights from the batch.

    Expects the data collator (e.g. DiffWeightedDataCollator) to populate
    batch["loss_weights"] as a Tensor[batch, seq_len] float32 aligned with
    labels. The weighted loss is:

        L = sum(per_token_CE * loss_weights * label_mask) /
            sum(loss_weights * label_mask)

    where label_mask = (labels != IGNORE_INDEX). This reduces to the standard
    CE loss when all loss_weights == 1.0, so the identity-under-uniform-weights
    invariant holds.
    """
```

Key points for implementation:
- Override `compute_loss(self, model, inputs, return_outputs=False, num_items_in_batch=None)` (HuggingFace Trainer API).
- Pop `"loss_weights"` from `inputs` BEFORE calling `model(**inputs)` — the model itself does not accept that kwarg and will choke if you leave it in.
- Compute per-token cross-entropy with `reduction="none"` so you can multiply by weights element-wise.
- Handle the shift: `logits[:, :-1]` vs `labels[:, 1:]` (causal LM shift).
- Apply `label_mask = (labels != -100)` so IGNORE_INDEX tokens contribute zero to both numerator and denominator.
- Return `(loss, outputs)` if `return_outputs=True` else `loss` — matching Trainer's contract.
- Defer trl imports inside the class body (INFRA-05 pattern) — or lazy-import at module scope alongside the existing factory.

Deferred-import reminder: this project stays CPU-importable for CI (INFRA-05). Current `build_diff_aware_sft_trainer` imports `trl.SFTTrainer` inside the function body. Define `DiffAwareSFTTrainer` either as (a) a class lazily created inside the factory after the trl import, or (b) use a lazy-import pattern at the top of `diff_loss.py` where the class references are resolved at instantiation. Option (a) is simpler but means the class isn't importable by name from `diff_loss`; option (b) is cleaner for testing and for making the class a named public API. Pick option (b) — the README and qodo thread both reference the class by name, so it should be importable.

Suggested pattern for option (b):
```python
def _get_sft_trainer_base() -> type:
    from trl import SFTTrainer  # noqa: PLC0415
    return SFTTrainer

# ... later, at top-level:
class DiffAwareSFTTrainer(_get_sft_trainer_base() if TYPE_CHECKING else object):
    ...
```
No — that's fragile. Better: keep the class defined lazily and expose it via a factory helper, OR use a proper deferred base-class pattern. Given this is a research codebase, a cleaner approach is to just move the `from trl import SFTTrainer` to module top level with a `try/except ImportError` guard for CPU-only CI:

```python
try:
    from trl import SFTTrainer  # type: ignore[attr-defined]
    _TRL_AVAILABLE = True
except ImportError:
    SFTTrainer = object  # type: ignore[misc,assignment]
    _TRL_AVAILABLE = False


class DiffAwareSFTTrainer(SFTTrainer):  # type: ignore[misc,valid-type]
    ...
```

This lets the class be importable from `diff_loss` on CPU (as a subclass of `object`) while still inheriting from real `SFTTrainer` on GPU. All existing tests for `diff_loss.py` run on CPU without trl installed — verify they still pass.

## Wire it into the factory

Update `build_diff_aware_sft_trainer` to instantiate `DiffAwareSFTTrainer` instead of `SFTTrainer`. The call signature is identical; only the class name changes.

## Update the docstring on `build_diff_aware_sft_trainer`

Change "instantiates `trl.SFTTrainer`" to "instantiates `DiffAwareSFTTrainer`" in the docstring. Keep the rest intact.

## TDD: tests to add

Add to `libs/model-training/tests/test_diff_loss.py`. Use TDD — write tests first.

**Unit tests (no GPU required; use a tiny stub model):**

1. **Identity invariant.** Construct a `DiffAwareSFTTrainer` with a tiny mock/stub base model (return fixed logits), inputs with all `loss_weights == 1.0`, labels of shape `[1, 4]`. Compute loss via both `compute_loss` and a manual reference CE-with-reduction="mean" on labeled tokens. Assert they match to float32 precision. Proves identity under uniform weights (the regression guard the rest of the codebase relies on).

2. **Weighted loss correctness.** Same setup, but `loss_weights = [[0.5, 1.0, 0.0, 2.0]]`. Manually compute the expected weighted mean; assert `compute_loss` matches.

3. **`loss_weights` popped from inputs.** Mock `model.__call__` to record kwargs it receives. Assert `loss_weights` is NOT in the kwargs (it's a trainer-only signal, not a model-forward arg).

4. **Missing `loss_weights` key.** If the batch doesn't contain `loss_weights` (e.g., raw SFTTrainer path), `compute_loss` should fall back to standard CE loss — no KeyError. Test this.

5. **Batch shape handling.** `[batch=2, seq=3]` with different weights per row — assert batch-wise reduction is correct.

**Integration test (if feasible):**
6. Smoke test that `build_diff_aware_sft_trainer(...)` returns an instance of `DiffAwareSFTTrainer`. May need to skip if trl isn't installed in CI — use `pytest.importorskip("trl")`.

Because SFTTrainer is a heavyweight HF class, use `pytest.importorskip("trl")` at the top of the integration tests. Unit tests 1-5 can operate on a minimal subclass that replaces the base with `object` during the test (use `monkeypatch` or a tiny fixture) so they don't need trl installed.

Actually — the cleanest approach: write `_compute_weighted_loss(logits, labels, loss_weights)` as a pure function inside `diff_loss.py` (no Trainer base dependency), have `DiffAwareSFTTrainer.compute_loss` delegate to it, and unit-test the pure function directly. This is the right separation: testable loss math + thin Trainer integration.

## Docs updates

- `libs/model-training/README.md` line ~30 and ~122 — the existing mentions of `DiffAwareSFTTrainer` are now accurate after this fix. Verify wording is consistent with the real class behavior.
- No need to update other docs — the architecture description didn't need to change.

## Acceptance

1. `DiffAwareSFTTrainer` class exists in `diff_loss.py` and is importable.
2. `_compute_weighted_loss` (or similar pure function) exists and is unit-tested.
3. Identity-under-uniform-weights invariant holds (test asserts).
4. `loss_weights` is popped from inputs before forward pass.
5. `build_diff_aware_sft_trainer` returns an instance of `DiffAwareSFTTrainer` (not vanilla `SFTTrainer`).
6. Docstring on the factory updated to reflect actual class instantiated.
7. `uv run pytest libs/model-training/tests/test_diff_loss.py -v` — all tests pass including the new ones.
8. `uv run pytest libs/model-training/tests/ -q` — full suite passes.
9. `uv run ruff check`, `uv run ruff format --check`, `uv run mypy libs/ services/` all clean.
10. `make docs-build` warning-free.
11. One commit titled something like `fix(pr28-review): implement DiffAwareSFTTrainer consuming loss_weights`.

## Before You Begin

Ask if:
- The deferred-import pattern for the trl base class is unclear.
- You're not sure whether to use `pytest.importorskip` or to stub the base class for unit tests.
- Anything about the expected weighted-loss math is ambiguous (e.g., what's the correct reduction for multi-sample batches — the spec says `sum / sum` per batch).

Don't overbuild. Keep the class focused on compute_loss override + loss_weights popping. No other Trainer hooks.

## Self-Review Checklist

- [ ] Identity invariant test passes on a known case with hand-computed expected value.
- [ ] `loss_weights` never leaks into `model(**inputs)`.
- [ ] Factory returns a `DiffAwareSFTTrainer` (not vanilla SFTTrainer) — asserted in an integration test if trl is available.
- [ ] Class is importable from `diff_loss` on a CPU-only machine (no trl) — verify with `uv run python -c "from model_training.diff_loss import DiffAwareSFTTrainer"`.
- [ ] Full test suite passes; mypy/ruff/format clean.
- [ ] docs-build warning-free.

## Report

- Status: DONE | DONE_WITH_CONCERNS | BLOCKED | NEEDS_CONTEXT
- Summary of the class + pure helper layout.
- Test counts (before vs after).
- Commit SHA.
- Any design tradeoffs you made (especially around the trl deferred import).

Do NOT push.

**Status: DONE**

**Summary**

- **`_compute_weighted_loss(logits, labels, loss_weights)`** — pure function in `diff_loss.py`. Applies the causal-LM shift (`logits[:,:-1]` vs `labels[:,1:]`), runs `cross_entropy(reduction="none")`, multiplies per-token CE by `loss_weights * label_mask`, returns `sum / sum` with a `1e-8` denominator clamp. No Trainer dependency — fully unit-testable.

- **`DiffAwareSFTTrainer(SFTTrainer)`** — defined at module top level. The `try/except ImportError` guard around `from trl import SFTTrainer` means on CPU-only machines (trl absent) the class subclasses `object` and remains importable. `compute_loss` pops `"loss_weights"` before the forward pass, delegates to `_compute_weighted_loss`, falls back to `outputs.loss` when the key is absent.

- **Factory** — `build_diff_aware_sft_trainer` now instantiates `DiffAwareSFTTrainer` (not vanilla `SFTTrainer`); the deferred `from trl import SFTTrainer` inside the factory body was removed (only `DataCollatorForCompletionOnlyLM` still imported there).

- **Tests** — 16 → 22 passed, 1 skipped (trl integration test, `importorskip`). New tests: identity invariant, weighted correctness, loss_weights not leaked, missing-key fallback, batch shape, return_outputs tuple.

- **Tradeoff on deferred import**: chose the `try/except` at module top level (option b from the spec) so `DiffAwareSFTTrainer` is a named public export importable by name. On GPU machines with trl installed it properly subclasses `SFTTrainer`; on CPU it subclasses `object` with a functional `compute_loss` that still runs correctly since the pure loss math has no Trainer dependency.

- **Commit**: `dd78cf8`
